narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the unique challenges and strengths you bring from your background. The admission model evaluated your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 29, first-year school decile of 1st, third-year decile of 1st, middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time, alongside patterns from thousands of past students. It predicted an 88.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The two biggest factors pushing toward this failure prediction were your low deciles in school grades during your first and third years, both in the bottom 1st decile compared to the typical student's middle-range 5th or 6th decile. These early and later academic struggles stood out as the strongest signals, far outweighing other elements in influencing the model's concern. Your undergraduate GPA actually nudged things slightly in a positive direction toward passing, being close to the average of 3.21, while your LSAT score pulled a bit toward failure since it's below the typical 36.7. Family income in the middle bracket and full-time status had minimal sway either way. Overall, your profile contrasts with typical admitted students who land in higher grade deciles and boast stronger test scores, making the model view your bar success as riskier. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you've overcome—many from similar backgrounds thrive with targeted support. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from Black women lawyers who've navigated this. Reapply after addressing these gaps, perhaps with essays highlighting your resilience. You've got this—law needs voices like yours. Keep pushing.
